What question is being answered?

This visualization answers: "How do route distance, seasonal demand, and aircraft deployment interact to determine which Emirates routes from Dubai are profitable, and what does the underlying cost structure reveal about why?"

The dataset contains 7,974 individual Emirates flight records across 30 destinations during 2024. The visualization surfaces several key findings: short-haul routes are systematically unprofitable (avg margin −26% vs. +23% for long-haul); peak-season short-haul flights average −10% margin while off-peak shorts hit −46%; load factor is the strongest predictor of profitability; and the A380 averages +23% margin vs. −8% for the 737-800 due to revenue-per-hour differences.

Design rationale

Bubble scatter plot: Each route is a bubble (x = avg revenue/flight, y = selectable metric, area = flight count, color = route category). This reveals the two-dimensional relationship between revenue scale and profitability that a bar chart would hide. The scatter supports geometric zoom and pan (scroll to zoom, drag to pan) via D3's zoom behavior, enabling users to inspect dense clusters of overlapping bubbles - particularly useful for the medium-haul routes that cluster near the center.

Brushable timeline: Monthly bars with a D3 brush for date range filtering, cross-linked to all other views. This implements coordinated multi-view interaction.

Dynamic query filters: Toggle buttons for route category, season, and aircraft type implement the dynamic queries paradigm from Shneiderman's HomeFinder. All filters compose together and with the brush.

Details on demand: Tooltips on hover + persistent detail panel on click + cost donut breakdown. This implements Shneiderman's information-seeking mantra: overview first, zoom and filter, then details on demand.

References & data sources

  • Data: Airline Route Profitability Dataset - 7,974 Emirates flights from Dubai to 30 destinations, 2024. (Kaggle)
  • Libraries: D3.js v7.9
  • Interaction design: Dynamic queries from Shneiderman's HomeFinder; NameVoyager for focused interaction design.

Development process

Developed individually over ~18 people-hours (Pranav - 9, Areeb - 9): data analysis (2h), design (2h), scatter plot (3h), timeline with brush (3h), sidebar filters and coordination (3h), cost donut and detail panel (2h), polish (2h), testing (1h). Most time was spent on multi-view filter coordination.
